denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This notice concerns the request to continue the intensive outpatient program (IOP) for depression submitted on your behalf. United has completed a medical necessity review and has determined that the requested continuation is not approved.

The requested duration of the program exceeds the course length supported under your plan's behavioral health guidelines. Our review applied the MCG behavioral health care guidelines in evaluating the requested continuation. Based on that review, the continued intensive outpatient program is not medically necessary because it does not meet United's criteria for medical necessity.

The documentation submitted does not support continuation at the requested duration. Additional sessions beyond the supported course length are therefore denied at this time.

If further treatment at this level of care is sought, updated clinical documentation may be submitted for a new review under the applicable guidelines.

You may request a copy of your claim file and the clinical criteria used in this determination at no charge. You have the right to appeal this determination within 180 days of the date of this notice by submitting a written request together with any additional records. You may also request a peer-to-peer discussion with a United medical director. If your appeal is denied, you may have the right to request an external review by an independent review organization, and you may bring a civil action under ERISA section 502(a) following completion of the plan's appeal process.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Behavioral Health Review
clinical_context: The patient is a 34-year-old woman with recurrent moderate major depressive disorder (F33.1) referred to an intensive outpatient program after two failed antidepressant trials: sertraline titrated to 200 mg daily for fourteen weeks and bupropion XL 300 mg daily for twelve weeks, each producing only partial response. Serial PHQ-9 scores were 19 in the fourth quarter of 2025 and 21 in the first quarter of 2026, both in the moderately-severe range and rising rather than improving despite the initial IOP block. Her treating psychiatrist documented persistent anhedonia, early-morning awakening with a roughly 4 a.m. wake time, and impaired work functioning that prompted a reduced-hours accommodation, with a safety plan in place and no current inpatient indication. The clinical record states that the patient was still mid-course in the evidence-based IOP curriculum and that discontinuation at the plan's standard duration cap would interrupt active treatment before measurable remission, with guideline-concordant care supporting extension when symptom scores remain elevated. The provider requested four additional weeks with reassessment by repeat PHQ-9.

strong_defenses: Procedurally complete notice: 180-day appeal window, free claim file and clinical criteria, peer-to-peer, external review, and ERISA 502(a) rights all disclosed
